commentBody:
"Hey, where's my stick to play this instrument?"
"Oh, the previous musicians took a bow."

("They're always stringing me along!")

Where were the rebuses and what was the theme?
After yesterday's comments, I KNOW THE FEELING.

I am a theatre major and a veteran; 
I prefer SET DESIGN to MORTAR FIRE.

Thank you, Barbara. SRSLY.

Wonderful puzzle, Barbara!  Thanks, Barry, for SRSLY.  
Tried to leave a comment after last Sunday’s beauty (which I really “liked”) but was emu’d.  Peace and good tidings to all.

This one was a weird one for me. During the entire fill, I squarely felt like I wasn’t on the same wavelength as Barbara and the editors, yet I slid in well below my average Friday time. I guess it’s due to the long, attainable answers which made up most of the fill, leaving only a smattering of discordant holes. Good bit of struggle, satisfying finish. Thanks Barbara!
Lovely puzzle! Totally solvable Friday!

Now that was one tough Friday puzzle that proceeded by fingerholds and toeholds but ultimately resolved itself. MY favorite kind of solve. It was a good deal longer than my Friday average, so the fun lasted longer. And there were plenty of cleverly deceptive clues that resisted for a long time. Looking forward to more puzzles from Barbara Lin!

Laughed out loud when Barry typed in SRSLY for me.

(Or is it chiseled in?)

I thought this was a very nice themeless Friday.  I got the right side reasonably easily, but the left side gave me a bit more trouble.  I tried hire for bring on board before LADE, which made it harder than it should have been to get those long downs.

A very smooth grid and solve. The shorter answers were a lot of gimmes for me so that took some of the excitement away from those excellent stacks (as well as some longer ones like TSETSE and DOOROPENER). Loved the original clue for EFILES and the fascinating observation about BIRDSOFPARADISE which led me astray for awhile because when I got BI I immediately tried BIO, and even when I got BIRDS couldn’t think how they could be FLORA. Interesting clue for ELLE Woods. The Legally Blonde movie is obviously more well known than the musical, but it was a great musical as well which I’ve seen several times, and the soundtrack is a hoot. This is the funniest song from it, apologies in advance if you are offended, it’s hysterical though, https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=5Kqo1pX8DEY&ra=m Surprised we had TOTE as both an answer and part of a clue, thought that was frowned upon. The BIGEASY reminded me of an under-appreciated movie of the same name with Dennis Quaid and Ellen Barkin, where I first heard the term. Actually I guess it’s a Dennis Quaid kind of day today since he also starred in the movie remake of DOA as well. I’ve always enjoyed him as an actor. A fine puzzle, over a bit too soon for my taste but it still added a few SMILELINES to my day. Thanks, Barbara!
Why has the crossword been shoved all the way to the bottom of the app?! This is the gold standard of puzzles.
Well worth the time.  Took me half as long as yesterday's puzzle.
I object to 62 across. AGNUS, yes. OPUS, yes. ADVOCATUS? Nope nope nope.
App issues are making this almost unplayable. I hope they fix it soon.

I don’t know how I got through this. 
Methodical solve working section by section, starting in NE and going counterclockwise. Thank goodness for connectors between sections. 

Excellent Friday. I hope folks didn’t find this too easy. 

But I’m 35000 ft in the air. Enjoying starlink.  Solving while concurrently watching World Cup.  And won’t admit to being drunk since the FAA? Might be reading this…I PLEADS IGNORANCE. 

I really impressed myself solving this.  Again. I hope this isn’t considered easy. 

Thank you Barbara and editors, for a proper? Friday?

Great Friday puzzle. I started off way too confident, thinking I was gonna smash my PB, but the bottom half had other ideas. Ended up finishing a minute under my Friday average.
Anyone else, KNOWTHEFEELING?

I personally would have liked it to put up more of a struggle. Just me. 
Happy T&T wedding eve (Lol)
@Sean. The OED gives both meanings of LOW as being different usages of the same word, “polysemy “ is the technical term for this.

I sent myself on a frustrating ten-minute detour on both sides of the grid by entering PLEADS InNOceNCE and THROb. The rest came easily once I figured out my mistakes there! 

I do wish to lodge one objection, though: the Brothers GIBB continued to produce top-ten hits through the 90s, and their last album came out in 2001. I ended up getting that answer by crosses because a clue looking for a 60s-80s act immediately ruled them out in my mind!

Long entries always scare me but these didn't give me too much trouble. That southeast corner, though ... 

I remembered the Brothers Four before GOATEE forced me to change it to GIBB. Now I have their lovely song Greenfields in my head. A nice song to fall asleep to.

The website was glitchy yesterday & again today. Fingers crossed for my streak, currently 1034. I updated my phone and iPad apps but I usually solve on my laptop, not on an app.

App continues to crash. No upgrade offered on the German AppStore 

Stuck with :€&@! software.  

The puzzle was fine. A decent Friday.

Concerning 46A...

I thought the coupling of "Fake news" and CANARD was interesting.

usually a morning solver, so fun to get into the conversation so early. enjoyed it a lot. After yesterday's storm of comments, I'm hoping this one proves less  controversial.

Was really looking forward to the tall ships for July 3-4, but with temperatures in three digits I'm not sure it's smart to try to see them in the East River on the 3rd. Heat domes are not our friends.  Stay safe and hydrated, everyone.

Why does "Mode, for one" solve to STAT? It's not covered by the column and I don't get it.

I know Google exists but please humor me: what's a NETIPOT? Is it that thing with a long spout that some people use to clear their nose?

(The crossing of those two entries was my last square in this challengingly enjoyable solve)
One of these days I'm going to remember I.M. PEI. Today was not that day 😅
TOTE is included both in a clue and as an answer. That’s typically not allowed, right?
12D: BIRD POO PARASITE  somehow seemed like a great answer for a minute there…

I echo all the positive comments on here so far, although I had to look up or reveal quite a lot: the curse of the spelling - moult/MOLT, the senator, old favourite SSNS. 

Tiny puzzles - KIT for a nurse? Not heard of nurses carrying bags about the wards.
And swag - seems odd for burglars to have a TOTE bag for their ill-gotten gains. 

Overall - excellent.

Nurse of 17 years here

What's a KIT? How do we tote it?

Hello! The crossword app has been glitching for 2 days, it shut down about 20 times while doing the puzzle. 
Took the joy out of it :-(

About 35D [Former Medicare IDs] SSNS

From the internet: "Medicare IDs were changed to protect beneficiaries from identity theft and fraud. A 2015 federal law mandated the removal of Social Security Numbers from all cards. The old Health Insurance Claim Numbers (HICN) were replaced with random, unique alphanumeric codes called Medicare Beneficiary Identifiers (MBI)."

Hands up: Does your doctor still ask you for your Social Security number? If so, why?

THIC. 

I loved some of the clues (EFILES was great) but i got totally stuck on NETIPOT, which I’ve never heard of, so i was harrumphing and disgruntled and gave up. Hopefully I’ll be gruntled again in time for Saturday’s puzzle.

For 57D [Brothers ___ (pop stars of the 1960s-80s)] I was thinking of the Brothers FOUR singing "Greenfields" but the Brothers GIBB is so much more fun, taking us through the highs and lows of "Saturday Night Fever." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VmHNMif29MU
Re: 11D: Aren't all supporters ALLIES?

More on 57D the brothers GIBB. Here's the whole "Saturday Night Fever" movie free online and the opening lyrics. https://pluto.tv/us/on-demand/movies/5bd34440603aedec59ff560c?utm_source=amazon&utm_medium=ossearch "Two, three, four One, two, three Well, you can tell by the way I use my walk I'm a woman's man no time to talk Music loud and women warm, I've been kicked around Since I was born And now it's all right, it's okay And you may look the other way But we can try to understand The New York Times' effect on man" What is that effect, anyway?
Fun Friday solve

Oh, gorgeous grid design, never used before in the Times.  

And it's so clear that Barbara spends time with each clue.  I love the misdirects and fuzziness – little things like [Choking cause, with "the"], and big things like [Makes a return that hits the net?].

Gorgeous answers, such as TAKES A BOW, PRESIDENTIAL RUN, CANARD, THROE, I KNOW THE FEELING, IMPULSE BUY, and MEWLS.

Barbara, you are one of the handful of constructors whose skillfully-made creations shimmer with quality, with a high level of art that makes me shake my head in wonder.  You do it every time, and I so look forward to what you come out with.  Thank you for another splendid, splendid outing.
NETIPOT almost was my undoing, but the crosses bailed me out (for me the lower right quadrant was basically the last area to be filled).

For me that was much more enjoyable than yesterday. Nice and chewy but doable. Annoyingly I was held up the longest with TEMPERA, I was so sure the second E should be a u that I discounted it. Also made SMOG and POEM invisible, despite being certain I knew what they should be. Doh. Added a good 10 mins to my solve time, I was so sure I ‘knew’ what I was talking about. That’ll learn me.

I also stuck with beards over GOATEE for too long. 

NETIPOT. Tried it once, felt like being waterboarded. Never again.
Doesn’t having TOTE in the clues and in the fill break some sort of crossword rule? I was surprised, especially since KIT could have been clued in a dozen different ways.

A very gently clued Friday with some nice scanners. My biggest problem was DOOR closER before DOOR OPENER. The movie The BIG EASY was one of my faves way back when and the soundtrack introduced me to the variety of the music from that part of our nation. But now, for a change of pace, Ladies and Gentlemen, the DEE GEES, a homage to the Brothers GIBB, the older three at any rate. RIP Taylor. https://youtu.be/p4DeaXf1FzE?si=h6VNZahFlIwUaZFc
Smooth, delightful puzzle, even if I didn't know netipot.

Good, tough Friday puzzle, for this solver.

The East proved to be the last to fall. 
NETI POT!

I tried a NETI POT...once.
It was around the same time as the atrocities at Abu Ghraib were hitting the news, and my NETI POT experience felt like self-imposed water boarding.
A friend told me that I was doing it wrong, and I'm sure that she was right, but...
Never again!
I needed this one after Thursday's debacle.
Why is the app STILL crashing even after the update??
Even after solving, I was baffled by 48D. So many random expressions and redundant like this nowadays. That's language, I guess.

For 66A, lifestyle for those who go both ways, I first entered BICOASTAL and thought that was pretty clever! A couple of downs didn't seem to fit, and for a while I thought the solution was BICURIOUS. Oh my, where are my pearls?

Not completely thrilled with BICULTURE, but it works.
Another good workout.  19% slower than average mostly due to some of the answers at the bottom and on the right side of the puzzle.  Do you know the feeling?

I don't often love a Friday puzzle. For me, they're usually like The Three Bears, i.e. so easy that I finish them too quickly, or so hard that I struggle mightily to complete them, which I usually do after raging at the constructor or editors. This one was just right. A little tough for a brief moment when I filled in PLEADS INNOCENCE, but saw the error of my ways because BIG EASY was a gimme. Loved the spanners. Thanks for a great job, Barbara!

I used to use a neti pot equivalent - a plastic squeeze bottle that comes in a kit with salt packets, to which I'd add either distilled or boiled, then cooled water. It was truly gross, though it did bring some relief. But the last time I used it, the result was a bloody nose, which I think I've only had two or three times in my life, so that was the end of that.

Quite a fine Friday puzzle. "Makes a return that hits the net?" for EFILES was very clever, as was "Bass player" for WOOFER. The latter I got immediately, but the former required a lot of crosses!

The long entries were all terrific, and the fill was very solid, with HITOUT being perhaps the only entry that seemed odd to me. Certainly familiar with "strike out" and "lash out", but not "hit out". But maybe that's just me.

Took me a bit over 25 minutes, and this one seemed just right for a Friday. My sense was that there were far fewer "gimme" clues than we've had in recent late-week puzzles. If so, I certainly hope that continues.

Great Friday puzzle: Seemed opaque at first, took a while, but figured everything out with no lookups or hints!! NETIPOT was one of my first entries, but then I doubted myself. YIPS was almost last entry to fall into place. Fun!
I literally untyped „tote“ when I saw it later in a clue.

Fell asleep with about half a grid after 25 minutes or so last night. Got back to it with fresh eyes this morning and still struggled, but less so. Good puzzle, never felt uncertain about anything, just a slow grind to figure it all out

Two nits:

(1) "Mode" is a statistic, not a STAT. Use RBI, or OBP, or almost anything else.

(2) Losing hair is "shedding". "Molting" is about feathers or, perhaps, exoskeletons.

Of course, "misdirection" excuses a lot, especially at the end of the week.
Fast and fun
This was a great puzzle. Thank you, Barbara!

What a fabulous puzzle. First run was looking bleak, then it all fell into place in a very satisfying manner. 

Well done.

Not expecting quite so much Hoo-raw over today's puzzle --as compared to the 1015+ Comments that eventually accumulated by the time I checked last night.  Goodness!

This puzzle from Ms. Lin had quite a few sneaky/clever misdirects that I enjoyed being fooled/baffled/delighted by!  

My favorite was 45D, where I had TEM---A and kept wanting TEMPURA despite its obvious ill-fitting-ness...plus I had THOU at 59D (and took out MEWLS)... oh, what fun!

I would have slapped my head when TEMPERA paints revealed themselves, but I can't afford the brain damage...  Those were the 'art supplies' of childhood ....

"Shelling" can be quite a bit more than MORTAR FIRE.  There were always howitzers in the parades--much more impressive than stumpy little MORTARs.  I wanted ARTILLERY ..but too long!

Typical tough Friday for me, but an enjoyable workout. Just had to work the crosses and ponder and things eventually fell together. And... two puzzle finds today. Both with somewhat similar tricks. First one a Sunday from December 6, 2009 by Louis Sabin with the title: "Plus Factor." Some theme answers in that one: SLIGHTFINGERED KNIGHTMARE WRIGHTHOOK TALLSTARTEAM TEAWAGON BLANDLORDS PLEADINGLADIES Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=9/23/1990&h=124a I'll put the other puzzle in a reply. ...
As a player myself, I really wanted 53 down to be Wooten. This one was written for me, my fastest Friday in a while but still fun.

I always enjoy Barbara Lin's late-week puzzles for the same reason I enjoy Robyn's.  There's no arcane trivia to wrestle with -- just a fresh, new way of looking at well-known in-the-language words and phrases.  The grids look effortless (although I'm sure they're not) -- with grid-spanners like PRESIDENTIAL RUN, PLEADS IGNORANCE, I KNOW THE FEELING and BIRDS OF PARADISE (what a lovely, curiosity-provoking clue for that entry!) smoothly incorporated.  You can expect clever and original cluing too: I especially liked the clues for PRESIDENTIAL RUN; E-FILES; IMPULSE BUY and ETAS.  (ETAS, which has been seen in like maybe 100,000 previous puzzles, is a reminder that even the most familiar entry can be clued in a way that's really different and really imaginative.

A word to solvers about IM PEI.  If it's a 5-letter architect, it's always IM PEI.  No matter what the building is or where it is, it's always IM PEI.  You're welcome.

A really elegant Friday themeless.  Today, I didn't miss Robyn Weintraub at at all.

Ok, it's time for Stupid Memories That Go Back More Than 50 years.

Does anyone remember Johnny Carson's "Karnak" bit?  Once every bit he'd utter the curse, "May the Bird of Paradise fly up your nose".

At some point that was also a gag song.  I think Carson can first, then the song.  I'm not sure if Carson stole it from someone.
Wonderful Friday. Thank you Barbara (and the editors).
I'm reading through the comments, and every time there is a new one, I get taken right to it, thus losing my place. Is it just me? It's annoying.
Well, now that the app is crashing over and over, the NYT has stopped nagging me every day to leave them a review in the App Store. Weird.

I don't need the constructor to mis-direct me, I can do that very well all on my own.  [Shelling}?  Sure that's pea related!

[Makes a return . . .]?  Oh, has to be tennis!

Those and learning there is a Mammoth NHL team made that section quite the workout.  

Nice one.  Thanks.
Crashing. Every. Time. Grrrr
48 Down was really the only bad clue/answer in this puzzle (HITOUT). Sort of easy for a Friday. It almost felt like a Wednesday...

Not my favorite but appropriate for a Friday. 

Rating:
7/10

Excellent puzzle! This is what a Friday should be.

My one nit is that TOTE appeared both as the answer to 20A and in the clue for 8D. I know there is no real rule against this sort of thing, but that doesn't mean it's a good idea. It's like microwaving fish in the office kitchen: technically it's not against the rules, but please don't.

Impossibly difficult, with several answers that skated past naive editors. In no universe does "Choking cause, with "the" translate to "YIPS". If - God forbid - you are at the absolute top of your game; with nary a trace of a "choke", you still can become possessed by a case of the yips. See 2nd baseman Steve Sax. From Google/AI: "...the yips"—is a psychological and neurological condition where a highly skilled athlete inexplicably loses the motor skills required to perform routine tasks."

Also, in no conceivable way does "Brothers ___ (pop stars of the 1960s-80s)" translate to "GIBB". The Bee Gees are the Bee Gees, full stop. Whover approved this may as well have cited the "The Brothers Karamazov" - at least the answer would have been accessible.

Not. fun. full. stop.

Moving on the floor now, babe, you're a BIRD OF PARADISE
Cherry ice cream smile, I suppose it's very nice

Ah, Duran Duran, my guilty pleasure. The Bee Gees, not so much. That was a very good spanner, by the way, and intriguingly clued. The clue for ELIA (9D) was a bit tortured, though.

I had to look it up, but the US military stopped stamping your SSN on your dog tags in 2015. It's been replaced by a randomly generated DoD ID number, also 10 digits; just one more thing to remember in boot camp.

It was bumper to bumper at the intersection of YEOH and HITOUT. 

Nothing to MEWL about.

I really didn’t understand why KIT was clued as “Nurse’s tote”. It just seems extremely imprecise so many people could carry a kit. In fact most of the nurses I see are carrying an iPad or laptop. Maybe there are some nurses who could enlighten me? Again not arguing about fairness it was easily crossed but it just seems like an inelegant clue.

Generally liked this puzzle but had "birds of a feather" which seemed to fit the clue. Did anybody else do that?  Also had "hire" instead of "lade".

And to the person who quibbled about the Brothers Gibb, any true fan would know that they've often been referred to that way.
Let's talk about no-lookup streaks. My longest is 110.  Right now I'm 48 with no look-ups. What is your longest streak with no lookups?

Ugh.  I failed this one with resounding thud.  Had about a third or more filled in, several of which were wrong.  Did not know YIPS and would have used THROES if enough spaces available.  For "Greets" I had NOD, had HIRE instead of LADE, and so on.  Can't criticize the author, this was one tough puzzle and very well (very cleverly) clued.
Themeless carries its own special bliss!
12D for clue and answer of the year.

Oh I could not disagree more with 9D ""Not many sounds in life, and I include all urban and all rural sounds, exceed in interest a knock at the door"
I detest knocks at the door. I am not a misanthrope, I just don't want solicitors for some cause that I don't support trying to persuade me that my mortal soul is on the line if I don't take a brochure; and I don't want my actual friends to just drop by in this era of a quick prior text to establish a visit. For the causes I do support, I don't prefer someone at the door to remind me. (Having myself gone door to door for causes, I'm aware that would-be donors don't like any approach: "don't call me! But also, don't write me. And don't knock on my door!" I am not quite hoist on my own petard, because I make my list of causes and affirmatively seek them out rather than needing to be asked.) 
This has given rise to a gentle controversy in my own household. I will often simply ignore a knock at the door. Even if the people on the other side can see that I am in the house. I don't bother looking up if I don't feel like talking to the visitors. Leave me alone, I'm doing the crossword puzzle. Members of my household say that I am being rude to not answer the door. I reply in kind that it's my house, and I have a right not to answer the door or acknowledge the banging. 
Weigh in if you like.

Well. I just finished because I kept needing to go back to right middle area. I had spew instead of spec, even though it was already used and I did not know the word canard. I 'plead ignorance'I was happy to figure out 'door opener' and 'set designs'. Loved the clueing for all the spanners. They were straight forward enough but made for fun and some smiles. 😊' I know the feeling' of a Friday success!
This was a fine puzzle but took me half an hour longer than yesterday’s puzzle
I was so sure of OKNOWIMLISTENING. It fit so many of the crosses! It really held me up until the end of the puzzle, when I looked up the Gobb brothers and learned my mistake.

A bar, bar, bar, bar, Barbara Lin
Bar, bar, bar, bar, Barbara Lin
Barbara Lin

Oops, sorry.  I listened to a lot of Beach Boy songs yesterday and got carried away.

Great puzzle with very entertaining clues.

WOOFER for [Bass player]

[Makes a return that hits the net?] for EFILES

There were more, but my earlier comment never posted and I can't remember what they were.

So if I don’t know what a word means - and I cannot ascertain the meaning from a root and/or crosses - does using the dictionary count as a “look-up?” Sometimes the definition is the answer (which feels not-so-great) and other times I’m still left befuddled. Not worried I’m “cheating” - who cares but me? - but I am curious what others think as I see the term “look-ups” frequently in comments. 

Great puzzle today! After the annoyingly long slog through rebus hell yesterday (even if you didn’t like it you’ve got to admire the construction!) today was just right for a Saturday lead-in.

Pretty easy for a Friday.  Really liked that there were no Natticks.  Made it easier to solve for clues like Villanelle.  Never heard the term before (TIL).  I did have PLEADSINNOCENCE for a while but the crosses helped with this.  Nice construction.

In my many years of doing crosswords this ranks amongst my least favorite. 

Just a joyless set of dubious stretches and tiresome groaners.

Pack up your troubles in your old KIT swag bag, and SMILELINE, SMILELINE, SMILELINE.

No SNITS here on my end, yes, SRSLY!  Well, like others, I didn't really love HITOUT, but no snits!

I did have several thises before thats, which I always like, especially when I think it's just going to be a standard crossword answer that we've seen before, but it turns out to be fresh! 

Kind of had a big hang up for a while in the lower left because on my initial run through, which yielded little, I put sugarbeet instead of BEETSUGAR. I somewhat recently wrote an impact report about sugar beets, so I was very happy to plunk that in there so early. I felt so smart I thought I would TAKESABOW

After I had to rip it out in service of the crosses, and saw BEETSUGAR emerge, I had to laugh at myself! 

Read the clue better, old girl!!

Anyhow, I thought it was a fun one with lots of good fresh, clever clues! I finished up just a hair or two slower than my average.  

Hmmm, maybe I'm MOLTing
Spent way too much time figuring out if I was spelling "philtrum" wrong."
Fun but very easy for a Friday, with a couple of look-ups.

I'm surprised there's no discussion of xwstats yet. Maybe I missed it. It's currently rating this one as Hard. My gut feeling is the rating should be at most Average. 

N.B. I'm not a huge fan of the site but it often comes up on Friday puzzles
An uphill battle the whole way through

poem made from words in today’s crossword puzzle
a/ i may go out the way of the volcano the stone the root tell me are you asleep? d/ really, really close but not you too? who knew the sounds of sleeping a/ know the feeling

Such a terrific puzzle. Thanks Barbara Lin, and thanks also for sticking with the concept for all those years.
And thanks, Sean McGowan for a great Wordplay column. I especially enjoyed the video you linked of all the DAPS. Have to remember to show it to my husband, as he's the one who watches sports all the time, and sports clues are usually the hardest for me.
The puzzle wasn't easy for me, but it was a lot of fun and gave me many aha moments. (Oh, so that's what a nasolabial fold is!!!) I did have some look-ups (my rules) but not as many as I thought there would be when I started earlier this morning. Often it was the umpteenth time I reread the clue at looked at the missing letters that the word or phrase or shortening came to me. Such a great feeling.
I finished yesterday's puzzle late at night and found it really tough. I admired it a lot though, and realized my aged brain doesn't remember much about U.S. history and geography as I'd like.
I always thought it was seemy, not seamy. Huh, learned something today.

Thanks to whoever fixed the bug in the web version where focus shifted away from the puzzle when you changed Up/Down direction or tried to fill in a rebus! At least for me on macOS Firefox, those functions are now working properly again, which lowers both my frustration and my solving time. (Finished today's puzzle in under my average Friday time, FWIW.)

I had POEt not POEM for the longest time, and I just wanted taRE to be 61D "Nothing But".  Kept modifying the I KNOW THE FEELING to force it in, but finally backed up and completed.

Also I know some song in the 1970s involving a Strawberry and a number was performed by the... "Brothers X" for 57D.  BEET SUGAR came easy somehow, so... "Brothers jamB" ?  "Brothers bomB" ?

Groaned with GOATEE finally jumped out (after putting the puzzle away for an hour or so) and GIBB.   The BeeGees for goodness sakes.

Then found "Strawberry Letter 23" by the Brothers Johnson, and fell down the rabbit hole of Shuggie Otis, Johnny Otis, Hank Ballard, Esther Phillips... kinda brought me back to why I do these puzzles in the first place.  They are stream of consciousness stimulators for me.  I'd have never even thought of Strawberry Letter 23 for maybe the rest of my life without this puzzle.

I had been getting a bit oppressed by maintaining my streak lately.   Nice reminder that there is some salutary brain reorganization that these puzzles stimulate.
The fill is nice but the cluing is atrocious

Late puzzle find. One of the oddest I've ever encountered. Can't imagine having much of a chance at this one. Anyway... a Sunday from May 27, 2001 by Dana Motley with the title: "A Matter of Degree." All the theme answers were clued in sequence as: "Start of a quote" "Quote, part 2" etc. Here are those answers: CANYOUIMAGINE DRFAHRENHEITANDDR CELSIUS TALKINGDRFAHRENHEIT WOULDSAYMANITSHOTIN HEREAND DRCELSIUS WOULDSAY ITSNOTTHATHOT Whew. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/27/2001&h=77a ....

Speaking of THROE, I puzzled over what possibly could be SET Db SIGNS. Perhaps high volume warnings?

⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️

Re 6A TAKESABOW That isn't how you spell SABOT https://music.youtube.com/watch?v=CotZfnKGWHM Otherwise, I CANARDly say enough about this Friday that had a SMILE LINE by LINE by LIN. Now really IMPEI shan't for more, tyvm!! PS
Michelle Yeoh from EEAAO, still a favorite movie.  I had "MORTAL FIRE" for 2 down for a while. Not sure what that means but could be a movie title.

Wow!! East became really difficult!!! I was at that all day!! 
Great puzzle 👏👏
Excuse me while I soak my bruised and tired self in Epsom salts. Todays puzzle put up one hell of a fight. Took me all day to get the SE corner. I'm bushed.

Despite getting hung up for …. just a minute on BIRDS OF PARADISE / CANARD / SET DESIGNS / THROE / SPEC / STAT and ROSEN I really liked the first 85% of this puzzle.

Cheers

A very pretty grid; I was impressed right away. Wasn't getting too far on my first pass around the clues and thought about putting the puzzle check on, but I have surprised myself by completing it with no look-ups, using the technique of just keeping at it! Sometimes I find stepping away and coming back later brings new thoughts to the things that stumped me before. Interesting the way the brain works.
For the longest time I denied myself TOTE because it was used in another clue - I would’ve assumed that was a style guide no-no.

Outstanding grid-spanners fer sure:

I KNOW THE FEELING of wanting to see the PRESIDENTIAL RUN... far away, very very far away.
Challenging puzzle! But why is the word "for" included at the end of the clue for 31 Across? I kept deleting the correct answer in an effort to solve for that question.

For me, the dividing line is whether their art furthers their politics aside from the mere fact of celebrity.
Easy case: Wagner. I happen to dislike his music per se but he was certainly a vile person who held indefensibly disgusting views of humanity. And his operatic works furthered the myths of Aryan purity.
Tougher case: Woody Allen. His personal life is beyond the pale but he always made me laugh. I used to think his art was not an articulation of the same views expressed in his life choices but latterly I have my doubts. Thinking of the teenage Mariel Hemingway in Manhattan, an otherwise tremendous film, makes me queasier every time i see it.

I didn't think of the Brothers Four, but should have, since I seem to be revisiting a lot of the music from my earliest days, and Greenfields was always a favorite. I played it a few months ago, and now I'm happy to have it playing in my head again. But really is a very sad song.

Once there were greenfields
Kissed by the sun
Once there were valleys
Where rivers used to run

Once there were blue skies
With white clouds high above
Once they were part of
An everlasting love

We were the lovers who strolled
Through greenfields

Greenfields are gone now
Parched by the sun
Gone from the valleys
Where rivers used to run

Gone with the cold wind
That swept into my heart
Gone with the lovers
Who let their dreams depart

Where are the greenfields that we
Used to roam

I'll never know what made you run away
How can I keep searching when dark clouds hide the day

I only know there's nothing here for me
Nothing in this wild world left for me to see

But I'll keep on waiting
'Til you return
I'll keep on waiting
Until the day you learn

You can't be happy
While your heart's on the roam
You can't be happy
Until you bring it home

Home to the greenfields and me
Once again

Written by: Terry Gilkyson, Richard Dehr, Frank Miller

In was quite proud of myself when I figured that one out. The term exists in Polish, too, sort of. "Kaczka dziennikarska" ("journalist's duck", and "canard" is French for "duck") is an old Polish name for fake news, a falsehood intentionally made public for effect.

@Andrzej That's fascinating. I never connected the unusual English word canard with the common (and tasty) French one. But if it is indeed derived from it, I wonder how a duck came to mean a baseless rumor, in either English or Polish!

Well, I'm a mathematician, but not a statistician. This one seemed off to me. The mode of a distribution is a statistic, and calling it a STAT seems very wrong. But it's Friday, so let's just call this "misdirection".

@joel88s

I've heard statystyka (statistics) in a mathematical context referred to as "stata" in Polish, even though we don't have the American penchant for shortening every other word.

@joel88s Geek alert!  Mode is a statistical (STAT) tool for measuring the most frequent occurence of a phenomenon and is used all the time in sports.  My dad, a baseball coach, diligently studied batting patterns of his and the opposing batters.  He wanted to know who swung early, late, went frequently for low balls, otuside balls, etc.   Typically, I hear the word STATS in this context but STAT is used by many people who study evidence-based practices.
@Jon There are no more common stats than mean, median, and mode.

@Times Rita

I will never, ever, EVER use a NETIPOT.
I wonder if Dr. Spock was responsible for this:

When we kids had bad colds, our mom would fill a sink with very warm-to-hot salt water and then force one's face down into the water, insisting that we "snook it up" ...sort of an "Introduction to Drowning."  I guess it worked, but to this day I will not put my face in the water in a swimming pool.

@Mean Old Lady .....  I am sorry for the trauma this caused you but the visual of gave me a great laugh this morning!  I imagine "Snook it up" will be in my brain and bring a laugh all day.  Thanks for sharing.  🤪

@Mean Old Lady

My mom did something similar; we had the sink of hot water, but a towel over our heads as a tent. We never actually touched the water. That was followed by hot soup to further the treatment. It worked great. As I got older, I found that just soaking a washcloth in hot water and laying it on my face and breathing did the trick.

My son uses the netipot. I don't want to know about it, or see it, or try it or, whatever. I know it's supposed to work well and many doctors recommend it, but...ew. No thanks.

Chicken soup, however, works every time. When I'm ill, my husband will go out and buy sick and I'll have it by dinnertime. When he's sick, I'll make it from scratch and he'll have it by lunchtime. I always make sure to have basic ingredients in the house, and I always freeze at least one serving, just in case.

I'd say wrong on both counts.

@Francis As you might imagine I have a personal interest here. Here's Google AI's take: "The iconic phrase did not actually originate with Johnny Carson. It was coined by his head writer, Marshall Brickman, during The Tonight Show. Brickman created the putdown—originally dubbed the "Carnac Saver"—as a fallback insult for Carson to use whenever the audience booed or groaned at a bad joke during his Carnac the Magnificent sketches. The phrase was immortalized in 1965 when songwriter Neal Merritt borrowed it from the show. Merritt used it as the title and chorus for a novelty song that became a massive hit for country music singer Little Jimmy Dickens." https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carnac_the_Magnificent

Mark,
My wife has the graduate degree in that field, but I know enough about words to know that "In sports, choking is the failure of a person, or team, to act or behave as well as anticipated or expected," and, as the clue states, the YIPS can certainly cause that. This is a crossword puzzle, not an applied physiology exam.
@Barry Ancona No need to get personal about it.

@Barry Ancona I think the issue may be that choking has been associated with mental mistakes, as described here: "https://www.peaksports.com/how-do-i-stop-choking-in-big-moments/ I remember the yips in golfing being attributed to this, on air, in sage tones when golfers were talking about other golfers who had them. Discovering the yips were neurological shifted treatment and hopefully tamped down the kinds of unhelpful advice athletes were hearing from others. It makes sense that what we mean when we say "choking" should expand beyond the assumption of mental mistakes, but I'm not sure everyone has made this shift.

@Barry Ancona
I'm sure you knew all this. But it is kind of sad to know these negative connotations are still out there.

What I mean by a "lookup" is getting information from any source whatsoever, including referring to any books or other printed matter, searching for an answer on the internet, and not asking anyone.

@Lynn
I enjoy learning too, but I also get a big shot of dopamine from 'not' having to look things up. It's a measure of how well my 77 year old brain is working. To me, looking things up cheapens the prize.

@Laura Stratton
Yeah, I think that is true for me, too. I'm 72 so it's also a measure of how up I've kept myself on the current cultural scene, new slang, etc.

@Lynn Agreed that it’s boring if Google just gives away the answer without any actual research. In the occasions when I turn to Google for help, I’ve learned to do so in a roundabout way by typing in what my guess is for the answer and then some other word to help put it in the right broad category (author, movie, etc—it’s usually a proper noun that catches me). Or I start to type a word related to the clue and immediately click on the Wikipedia entry to circumvent any obvious spoilers from Google.

@Lynn
Sometimes it takes me more than two hours to solve a puzzle, sometimes going to bed and finishing it in the morning. @Asher B. I would imagine it was more true when he wrote it in 1823 than it is now.

@Asher B.

I couldn't agree with you more. Who knows what's going on behind my door?  Maybe I'm not in the mood for company, maybe I'm "busy", maybe I'm otherwise engaged or not dressed, or sleeping, or putting a cake in the oven.

Like you, I have no problem with completely ignoring a knock.  Let them wonder.    

Call, email, bump into me on the street, but do not knock on my door!

@Asher B.

I imagine a knock on the door in early 19th century England had much different implications than one today.

@Asher B.

Though I acknowledge that times are different from when that was written, I totally agree with you here! 

What I think is rude is dropping by unexpectedly. I can assure you I am never prepared for company unless I know they are coming, and I just happen to really love company! But please, let me prepare. 

For the last many years, we live in a secure building, so it's not as much of a hassle as when we used to live in a house but I won't open the door to a knock unless I'm expecting it.

I remember one time, when we still lived in the house, I was at the sink, which had a window overlooking the door and driveway area. And someone knocked. I could see it was a van man trying to sell meat, which, well, heck no! I'm not buying your van meat! 

Anyhow, the guy looked up and saw me standing there and pointed to the door.  I just shook my head and waved goodbye. 

My husband comes from a culture that is very much about the drop in! We go back to his origins once a year and after all of these years going, I still struggle to abide the drop in...  But there it's not rude at all, so what are you going to do!?

@Asher B.
Usually anyone I know will text before they come to the door. The last time someone actually knocked, I was taking a soak in the tub, so ignored it. The knock came again and again, so finally I wrapped myself in a towel, took a peek out of the window, and saw a police uniform.

Opening the door slightly, it turned out the disturbance call came from a house number that does not exist on our street, so I suggested she check a similarly named street in our town.

As she turned to leave, I said, "Thank goodness you were a woman." She said, "Well now you can get back to, um, whatever it was that you were doing."

WHAT DID SHE THINK I WAS DOING?
